one_line_takeaway: MirrorMark shows that a measure-preserving 'mod-1 mirroring' symbol-to-randomness mapping -- rather than the cyclic-shift or interval mappings used by prior multi-bit watermarks -- creates stronger matched-vs-mismatched score separation, yielding higher bit accuracy and detection at near-zero text-quality cost, though robust payload recovery under paraphrasing remains unsolved for all compared methods.
key_finding: On the main 36-bit / 54-bit payload comparison at 300 tokens (Table 3), MirrorMark's Gumbel-max instantiation achieves AUC=1.0 and TPR@1%FPR=1.0 at both payload sizes, with bit accuracy 0.9835 (36 bits) and 0.9683 (54 bits), while perplexity (7.05 / 7.18) stays close to the non-watermarked baseline (7.28). By contrast RSBH reaches comparably high detection (AUC 0.9998/0.9989, bit acc up to 1.0/0.9928) but at the cost of much higher perplexity (32.90 / 32.82, roughly 4.5x the non-watermarked baseline), and StealthInk preserves low perplexity (7.82/7.90) but has markedly weaker detection (TPR@1%FPR 0.852/0.890) and bit accuracy (0.8896/0.8415) than MirrorMark. In the controlled ablation isolating only the mapping rule (same Gumbel-max sampler, H=1), MirrorMark's mirroring map improves bit accuracy over ThreeBricks' cyclic-shift map, with the gap widening as symbol size m increases, and a parallel mirroring-based DiPmark variant outperforms StealthInk's interval mapping under permutation-based reweighting. Theoretical EER estimates (Table 2) closely track empirical EER: Gumbel-max empirical 0.0010 vs. asymptotic 0.0008; tournament empirical 0.0020 vs. asymptotic 0.0028. Under copy-paste robustness (Table 4, 400 tokens, 36 bits), MirrorMark's Gumbel-max variant keeps AUC=1.0 and TPR@1%FPR=1.0 through epsilon=0.4, versus RSBH collapsing from TPR@1%FPR=1.0 (epsilon=0) to 0.085 (epsilon=0.2) and 0.005 (epsilon=0.4). Under paraphrasing (Table 5), all methods' bit accuracy degrades to near chance (roughly 0.51-0.57 across schemes), but MirrorMark retains higher detection AUC/TPR@1%FPR than MPAC, RSBH, and StealthInk, e.g. Gumbel-max m=2 reaches AUC=0.9306 vs. MPAC 0.5743, RSBH 0.3414, StealthInk 0.5188. The paper explicitly frames this as 'tamper-evident provenance detection' surviving where fine-grained payload recovery does not.
core_contribution: MirrorMark is a mapping-centric approach to multi-bit LLM watermarking that decouples the 'symbol mapping rule' (how an embedded message transforms a detector-reproducible pseudorandom object zt) from the 'base watermarking sampler' (how that object is used to sample or reweight the next token). The paper's own new construction is 'mod-1 mirroring': each m-bit symbol M is assigned a pivot psi_M = M/2^(m+1) evenly spaced on the unit interval, and the sampler's pseudorandom value u is transformed as Psi(u; psi_M) = (2*psi_M - u) mod 1. This map is bijective and measure-preserving, so it preserves the uniform distribution required by distortion-free base samplers (proved in their Appendix E), and it makes the matched-message score and mismatched-message scores 'complementary' (evidence for the true symbol is anti-evidence for wrong symbols), unlike prior cyclic-shift (DISC, ThreeBricks) or contiguous-interval (StealthInk) symbol mappings. To support practical multi-bit payloads, the paper also introduces the Context-Anchored Balanced Scheduler (CABS), which assigns each eligible generation step to one of H message positions by picking the currently least-populated position within a context-anchored 'frame' (frame boundaries determined by hashing a window Q of W tokens), resetting position counts at each new frame. This is designed to both balance evidence across positions and localize the effect of insertion/deletion edits to a bounded region (max_len + W tokens) rather than desynchronizing the whole sequence. The authors instantiate MirrorMark on top of two existing distortion-free zero-bit samplers -- Gumbel-max sampling (from Aaronson & Kirchner's AA scheme) and tournament sampling (from Google's SynthID/Dathathri et al.) -- and derive closed-form asymptotic equal-error-rate (EER) expressions for both instantiations in the single-position (H=1) case.
threat_model: The setting is LLM output provenance/attribution, not adversarial ML security in the exploit sense. A model provider embeds an m-bit-per-position payload (e.g., model identity, generation time, usage context) into generated text via a keyed pseudorandom mapping during sampling; a detector holding the secret key must (a) decide whether a given text was watermarked (binary detection under a false-positive-rate constraint) and (b) recover the embedded multi-bit payload (message decoding), all without needing access to the LLM's output distribution at detection time. The adversary considered is a downstream party who edits or transforms watermarked text to evade detection or corrupt payload recovery: the paper explicitly evaluates copy-paste attacks (mixing in a fraction epsilon of non-watermarked text at fixed total length) and paraphrasing attacks (using a Pegasus-based rewriting model), and separately stress-tests robustness to insertion/deletion/substitution edits in an ablation of CABS's frame parameters. There is no adversary attacking model weights, prompts, or training data -- the threat model is purely about watermark evasion/removal and payload corruption by text-level edits.
limitations: The paper's own conclusion and Section 5.3 state that reliable payload recovery under strong paraphrasing remains an open problem: bit accuracy collapses to near-chance levels for every method tested (including MirrorMark) once text is paraphrased, even though detection AUC stays elevated. The m-bit mirroring construction cannot achieve exact pairwise complementarity for all incorrect messages simultaneously once m>1 (only even spacing/maximal worst-case separation, proven in an appendix not captured in this extraction) -- an explicit design tradeoff the authors note. The Bayesian tournament detector (Tour-Bayes) requires training a separate detector per configuration, which the paper flags as an added cost. Evaluation is limited to a single base model (LLaMA2-7B) and a single text domain (C4 RealNewsLike news-style text); no cross-model or out-of-domain generalization results are visible in the paper. the paper (first 22 PDF pages) did not reach the appendices covering the 72/90-bit stress tests, cross-language adaptation, and the CABS parameter sensitivity tables in full detail, so this review reports only the headline results explicitly stated in the main text.

datasets_used: C4 (RealNewsLike subset), 500 prompts sampled for main evaluation and for EER validation
quantitative_results: Table 3 (300 tokens, 36 bits): Gumbel-max AUC=1.0, TPR@1%FPR=1.0, Bit Acc=0.9835, Perplexity=7.0486 vs. Non-Watermark perplexity 7.2784
Table 3 (300 tokens, 54 bits): Gumbel-max AUC=1.0, TPR@1%FPR=1.0, Bit Acc=0.9683, Perplexity=7.1751
Table 3 (300 tokens, 36 bits): RSBH AUC=0.9998, TPR@1%FPR=0.9980, Bit Acc=1.0000, Perplexity=32.8955 (vs baseline 7.2784)
Table 3 (300 tokens, 36 bits): StealthInk AUC=0.9892, TPR@1%FPR=0.8520, Bit Acc=0.8896, Perplexity=7.8241
Table 2: Empirical EER Gumbel-max=0.0010 vs asymptotic 0.0008; Empirical EER Tournament=0.0020 vs asymptotic 0.0028
Table 4 (400 tokens, 36 bits, copy-paste eps=0.4): Gumbel-max AUC=1.0, TPR@1%FPR=1.0, Bit Acc=0.9328; RSBH AUC=0.8455, TPR@1%FPR=0.0050, Bit Acc=0.6038

Table 5 (paraphrasing, 36 bits/400 tokens): Gumbel-max m=2 AUC=0.9306, TPR@1%FPR=0.5780; MPAC AUC=0.5743, RSBH AUC=0.3414, StealthInk AUC=0.5188; bit accuracy for all multi-bit methods falls to roughly 0.51-0.57 (near chance)
RSBH baseline uses bias parameter delta=6.0 per its original design, cited by MirrorMark authors as the cause of its large perplexity inflation
baselines_compared: RSBH (Qu et al. 2024, symbol-dependent cyclic shift + error-correcting codes)
StealthInk (Jiang et al. 2025, contiguous-interval mapping over context-seeded permutation)
ThreeBricks (Fernandez et al. 2023, cyclic-shift multi-bit extension of AA/KGW, used only in the controlled mapping-rule ablation)
